description: I have recently tried mongodb on nodejs, when I created my project on local PC on windows 7 64bit, it was running well. But then I decide to switch to our distance server which runs a windows server2003. I installed the nodejs 32bit version as well as the mongodb 32bit version. then when I use "npm install mongodb" to install the driver, I got the belowing info ( see in the errorlog_npm.txt). I still went on to start the mongodb server on port 6001 with the command "mongod --dbpath D:\mongoData --port 6001", then use the mongodb shell and it was running well.("one connect open" showed in the mongodb service) But when I start my nodejs service, nothing happend in the mongodb(no connection was open). When I call db.open() function , I got "MongoError: connect Unknown system errno 10042", when I try to use collection.distinct() in the db.collection()'s callback function, it shows"MongoError: topology was destroyed" ( I suppose there is no connect between node driver and the mongodb) How do I fix this probleme?

summary: The API example for collection.createIndex shows Db.createIndex functionality.
description:
In the API documentation for node-mongodb-native v2.0, a few of the examples for Collection.createIndex actually show the usage of Db.createIndex.

Specifically example 1 and 4, found here: http://mongodb.github.io/node-mongodb-native/2.0/api/Collection.html#createIndex

The usage and parameters for the createIndex function example refer to:
https://github.com/mongodb/node-mongodb-native/blob/2.0/lib/db.js#L883

rather than: https://github.com/mongodb/node-mongodb-native/blob/2.0/lib/collection.js#L1267

I wouldn't mind putting in a PR to fix this myself, but I cant find where the docs for this project reside..

summary: Migration from 1.4x to 2.x: API broken: Identical function names have DIFFERENT result values
description:
Hello,
Migrating from 1.4.19 to 2.0.40. The "update" function now returns a different result ! the previous result is contained in result.result ...

1. http://mongodb.github.io/node-mongodb-native/2.0/meta/changes-from-1.0/
doesn't state any such changes. Beside the fact that such changes are terrible at least they should be properly documented!

2.  looking thru History.md on github/node-mongo-native

I noticed the following:

2.0.0-alpha1 2014-09-08
....
-remove third result on update and remove and return the whole result document instead (getting rid of the weird 3 result parameters)
Might break some application
-Returns the actual mongodb-core result instead of just the number of records changed for insert/update/remove
....

Please can you EXACTLY specify which functions of the 1.4 API have been changed in the 2.0 API and what action is to be taken to a program using the 1.4 API so that it can use the 2.0 API without getting "broken".

Thanks a lot
Ognian

summary: Timestamp deserialization does not seem to work properly
CRUD Callback Documentation Incomprehensive
description:
There are many methods of the Collection class that do not go into enough detail regarding the callback result.

Consider the {{Collection.updateMany}} ([doc link|http://mongodb.github.io/node-mongodb-native/2.0/api/Collection.html#updateMany]) method. The callback is documented to take on the form {{Collection~writeOpCallback}} ([doc link|http://mongodb.github.io/node-mongodb-native/2.0/api/Collection.html#~writeOpCallback]) which describes the following data model:

{noformat}
{
error: MongoError
result: Collection~WriteOpResult
}
{noformat}

{{Collection-WriteOpResult}} depicts the following data model:
{noformat}
{
ops: Array.<object>
connection: object
result: object
}
{noformat}

By this point, it is understood the callback arguments for {{Collection.updateMany}} are as follows:

{noformat}
error: MongoError
result:
	ops: Array.<object>
	connection: object
	result: object
{noformat}

In fact, the *actual* callback arguments are as follows:
{noformat}
error: MongoError
result:
	result:
		ok: boolean
		nModified: integer
		n: integer
	connection: Server.connections()[x]
	matchedCount: integer
	modifiedCount: integer
	upsertedId: object
		index
		_id
	upsertedCount: integer
{noformat}

What the documentation describes and the actual result are not completely aligned, and, worse-yet, the documentation leaves out key properties and their associated hierarchy.

This is just one example of many. I have tested a handful of Collection methods: insert, insertOne, insertMany, remove, deleteOne, deleteMany, updateOne (with and without upsert), updateMany, update (with and without multi), findAndModify, findOneAndUpdate (with and without returnOriginal), and findOneAndDelete. All of the aforementioned methods are at least lacking the description of properties, and many of them do not align precisely with the callback and result object definitions.

I have attached a sample script [^crud-ops-run.js] that I used to investigate the outcome of the callbacks. I have also attached the logged output [^crud-ops-test.out], and my crude/psuedo-yaml representation of the result models [^crud-ops-structure.txt].

The documentation for these important and often-used methods is confusing and incomplete. The examples hint towards some of the non-defined (in the formal documentation at least) properties, but they are scattered at best.

summary: API definitions for DB events (reconnect, parseError, etc)
Update APM spec to filter out sensitive commands
Make a direct connection to a replicaset member work
If connectWithNoPrimary is true then primary-only connection is not allowed
description: If you pass connectWithNoPrimary true and if you have a primary-only connection it will not be considered "connected". I believe this is just because of a tiny bug in mongodb-core. I made a PR to fix the issue: https://github.com/christkv/mongodb-core/pull/44

summary: Passive secondaries are not allowed for secondaryOnlyConnectionAllowed
description:
When connecting to a replicaset passive secondaries are not counted when obeying `secondaryOnlyConnectionAllowed`. In replset_state.js in mongodb-core if the server is passive it doesn't check to see if `secondaryOnlyConnectionAllowed` is set and true, it only does that for non-passive secondaries. There doesn't seem to be any reason why a passive secondary wouldn't be allowed to be the only connection.

Also, the info log is incorrect for non-passive secondaries, it still says "promoting to passive" instead of "promoting to secondary".

summary: findAndModify take a write concern
Synchronous operations
Simultaneous calls findOneAndUpdate with same query condition create several documents instead of one
description:
Clone https://github.com/amurchick/mongo-parallel
Install modules - npm install
Run script - bash run.sh
(MongoDb config - mongod.conf)

Script simply simultaneous calls findOneAndUpdate 20 times with same query:
findOneAndUpdate(
	{hash: hash, ts: ts},
	{
		$set: {expires: new Date(Date.Now() + 60 * 1000)},
		$inc: {count: 1}
	},
	{upsert: true}
)

After this expect one document like this:
{ hash: '123',
    ts: 1440322487,
    expires: Sun Aug 23 2015 16:35:47 GMT+0700 (ICT),
    count: 20 }

Instead of expected, created from 2 to 6 documents (script prints it) like this:
[ { hash: '123',
    ts: 1440323382,
    expires: Sun Aug 23 2015 16:50:42 GMT+0700 (ICT),
    count: 16 },
  { hash: '123',
    ts: 1440323382,
    expires: Sun Aug 23 2015 16:50:42 GMT+0700 (ICT),
    count: 1 },
  { hash: '123',
    ts: 1440323382,
    expires: Sun Aug 23 2015 16:50:42 GMT+0700 (ICT),
    count: 1 },
  { hash: '123',
    ts: 1440323382,
    expires: Sun Aug 23 2015 16:50:42 GMT+0700 (ICT),
    count: 1 },
  { hash: '123',
    ts: 1440323382,
    expires: Sun Aug 23 2015 16:50:42 GMT+0700 (ICT),
    count: 1 } ]

summary: Leaks global variable Promise
description:
With the new promise support, `es6-promise` leaks the global variable `Promise`.  Even when specifying a promise library to use, the `new Db` call [here](https://github.com/mongodb/node-mongodb-native/blob/2.0/lib/mongo_client.js#L245) will always trigger `es6-promise` to be run and leak the variable.  There's a related PR on `es6-promise` that may or may not go through: https://github.com/jakearchibald/es6-promise/pull/126

It seems that this was introduced in 2.0.36 and remains an issue through 2.0.42.

summary: forceServerObjectId is not considered during insertion
description:
version: 2.0.42

>lib/collection.js
-------insertMany function-line 435: options is not filled with forceServerObjectId property from collection properties . (we need to explicitly add it to insertMany query options)
>>>> I fixed it by using self.s.db.options.forceServerObjectId instead of options.forceServerObjectId
------- insertDocuments function-line 613,: insertDocuments don't use forceServerObjectId option from collection properties.
>>>> I fixed it by adding a condition on self.s.db.options.forceServerObjectId


>lib/bulk/ordered.js
-------OrderedBulkOperation.prototype.raw - line 325/328/334: don't use forceServerObjectId from collection properties
>>>> I fixed it by using this.s.collection.s.db.options.forceServerObjectId!==true;
-------OrderedBulkOperation.prototype.insert-line 353:don't use forceServerObjectId from collection properties
>>>> I fixed it by using this.s.collection.s.db.options.forceServerObjectId!==true;


>lib/bulk/unordered.js
-------UnorderedBulkOperation.prototype.raw: line 374/377: don't use forceServerObjectId from collection properties
>>>> I fixed it by using this.s.collection.s.db.options.forceServerObjectId!==true;
-------UnorderedBulkOperation.prototype.insert: line 308: don't use forceServerObjectId from collection properties
>>>> I fixed it by using this.s.collection.s.db.options.forceServerObjectId!==true;

description:

After reading the code I find that GridStore actually ignores readPreference from connection string while writeConcern is inherited from there. This seems very much like a bug. Is it expected behavior?

The result object actually returned contains an insertedId property but no insertedIds property

summary: findOne and findOneAndUpdate when query has undefined value
description:
db.collection.findOneAndUpdate({uid:undefined})
causes an update of the random record. 
Instead, it should behave the same as
db.collection.findOneAndUpdate({uid:null})
which works as expected since there are no records with uid:null.

Similar issue with db.collection.findOne({uid: undefined}) - returns result equivalent to db.collection.findOne({})

Tailiable cursor fails silently on empty capped collection
description:
Hello,

When creating a tailable cursor on an empty collection, it fails silently.

Reproduction:
https://gist.github.com/yurynix/936786a68700c32ccb5f

When the above code is running, in mongo shell I execute:
> use myproject;
switched to db myproject
> show collections;
capped_test
> db.capped_test.insert({hello: 'world'});
WriteResult({ "nInserted" : 1 })
> db.capped_test.insert({hello: 'world'});
WriteResult({ "nInserted" : 1 })
>

However, the tailing cursor doesn't pick it up.

If I understand correctly this is a limitation of mongodb itself, however I would expect the driver to raise exception / emit error event on the stream.

summary: Order of sort fields is lost for numeric field names
description:
Order of sort fields is lost for numeric field names. Consider this example:

{{collection.find({}, { sort: [['2', 1], ['1', 1]] })}}

The expected result would be to first sort on field {{2}}, then sort on field {{1}}. Instead, it is first sorted on field {{1}}, then on field {{2}}.

The reason is that the driver converts the array of sort fields into an object in the method _formattedOrderClause_ in _utils.js_ (called at [http://mongodb.github.io/node-mongodb-native/2.0/api/lib_collection.js.html#line346]). Here the order is lost, because numeric property names are not -necessarily- enumerated in insertion order:

{quote}
ECMA-262 does not specify enumeration order. The de facto standard is to match
insertion order, which V8 also does, but with one exception:

V8 gives no guarantees on the enumeration order for array indices (i.e., a property
name that can be parsed as a 32-bit unsigned integer).
{quote}
([https://code.google.com/p/v8/issues/detail?id=164])

summary: GridStore.stream() returns an invalid Writable Stream
Connection started returning failed authentication today, after 2+ years
GridStoreStream & GridStore open
description:
Should we call GridStore.open() before using GridStore.stream() ?

Currently the code has inconsistent behavior:
* stream.pipe() calls gs.open() itself (even if already open, is it an issue? at least no visible issue, maybe just useless server queries)
* stream.write() calls gs.open() if not already open (checks gs.isOpen)
* stream._read() never calls gs.open(): if we don't call it manually, and don't use stream.pipe() it will break (see attached files)

Since we probably don't want to break anything, I think it would be best to add open if not yet open on all methods of GridStoreStream, for consistency; or document the fact that it's required to open the file, and remove useless open() (at least in pipe()).
